content.problemStatement:
Abdelmohaymn gives you a string SSS of length NNN that contains only **uppercase** and **lowercase** English letters.

He also gives you two integers xxx and yyy, meaning that the letters `'a'` to `'z'` have values [x,x+1,x+2,…,x+25][x, x+1, x+2, \ldots, x+25][x,x+1,x+2,…,x+25] (in order), and the letters `'A'` to `'Z'` have values [y,y+1,…,y+25][y, y+1, \ldots, y+25][y,y+1,…,y+25] (in order).

For example, the value of `'e'` is x+4x+4x+4, and the value of `'X'` is y+23y+23y+23.

It is guaranteed that ∣x−y∣≥26|x-y| \geq 26∣x−y∣≥26.

You can perform the following two operations on SSS:


Choose two characters c1c_1c1​ and c2c_2c2​. Let their values be vc1v_{c_1}vc1​​ and vc2v_{c_2}vc2​​ respectively.

Let fc1f_{c_1}fc1​​ denote the frequency (the number of occurrences) of c1c_1c1​ in SSS.

Then, if fc1>0f_{c_1} \gt 0fc1​​>0 and 0≤vc1−vc2≤fc10 \leq v_{c_1} - v_{c_2} \leq f_{c_1}0≤vc1​​−vc2​​≤fc1​​, do the following:

Delete **all** occurrences of c1c_1c1​ from SSS.
Append a single copy of c2c_2c2​ to SSS.


Rearrange the string SSS as you like.
Find the smallest string (by value) you can obtain by performing these two operations several (possibly, zero) times, in any order.

String AAA is smaller than string BBB by value if and only if one of the following holds:


AAA is a prefix of BBB, but A≠BA \neq BA=B; or
Let iii be the smallest index such that Ai≠BiA_i \neq B_iAi​=Bi​.

Then, vAi<vBiv_{A_i} \lt v_{B_i}vAi​​<vBi​​ should hold: that is, at the first differing character, AAA should have a smaller value.

Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line will contain three space-separated integers NNN, xxx, and yyy — the size of SSS, the value of `'a'`, and the value of `'A'`, respectively.
The second line will contain the string SSS.




Output Format
For each test case, output a single string on a new line: the smallest string (by value) that can be obtained by performing the given operations several times.
constraints: SSS contains only uppercase and lowercase English letters, that is, the characters `'a'` through `'z'` and the characters `'A'` through `'Z'`.
The sum of NNN over all test cases doesn't exceed 5⋅1055\cdot 10^55⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The frequency of character 'a' is 5 and its value is 777777.
Choose c1=c2=’a’c_1 = c_2 = \text{'a'}c1​=c2​=’a’ (valid because 0≤77−77≤50 \leq 77-77 \leq 50≤77−77≤5), which deletes all occurrences of it and replaces it with a single occurrence.
This is the smallest string possible.
Test case 222: Perform the following operations:

Choose c1=’e’c_1 = \text{'e'}c1​=’e’ (with value 101101101 and frequency 555) and c2=’c’c_2 = \text{'c'}c2​=’c’ (with value 999999). Since 0≤101−99≤50 \leq 101-99 \leq 50≤101−99≤5, this is a valid move, and all the 'e's are replaced with a single 'c'.
Now, S=S = S= Baccc.
Choose c1=’c’c_1 = \text{'c'}c1​=’c’ and c2=’a’c_2 = \text{'a'}c2​=’a’, to obtain S=S = S= Baa.
Choose c1=c2=’a’c_1 = c_2 = \text{'a'}c1​=c2​=’a’ to obtain S=S = S= Ba.
Choose c1=’B’c_1 = \text{'B'}c1​=’B’ and c2=’A’c_2 = \text{'A'}c2​=’A’ to obtain S=S = S= aA.
Rearrange the string to obtain S=S = S= Aa, which is the best we can do.

Test case 333: Choose c1=’A’c_1 = \text{'A'}c1​=’A’ and c2=’a’c_2 = \text{'a'}c2​=’a’ to obtain S=S = S= a.
